content:
Modern neural networks inherit their **layered layout** from the **McCulloch–Pitts neuron** (1943) and Rosenblatt's **perceptron** (1957): a unit that thresholds a weighted sum of inputs. Early models were **single-layer** — one output decision boundary.

The **multilayer perceptron** idea (hidden layers between input and output) emerged in the 1960s–80s as researchers sought **nonlinear** decision surfaces. Rumelhart, Hinton, and Williams (1986) popularized **backpropagation** for training these stacks, making depth practical.

As hardware scaled, **width and depth** became explicit design choices: wider hidden layers for capacity, deeper stacks for hierarchical features. Today's frameworks (PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX) still expose layers as the primary building block — each implemented as matrix operations over batches of activations.

timeline.label: McCulloch–Pitts neuron (1943)
timeline.description: Formal model of a binary unit combining inputs with weights — ancestor of the modern neuron compute unit.

timeline.label: Perceptron (1957)
timeline.description: Rosenblatt's trainable single-layer network; one output neuron, no hidden tier.

timeline.label: Backpropagation for MLPs (1986)
timeline.description: Efficient gradient computation through stacked layers made multilayer networks trainable at scale.

timeline.label: Framework era (2010s–present)
timeline.description: Layers exposed as nn.Linear / Dense modules; width and depth are first-class hyperparameters in every major DL stack.
